context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Multi-level Planning and Scale

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of power and legitimacy in multi-level planning? Is the State a site or an actor in multi-level planning? What is the role of resources in multi-level planning? How do collaboration and resistance happen in multi-level planning efforts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on this reflection about multi-level planning tbh. idk what “power and legitimacy” really mean here, and whether the state is supposed to be a site or an actor.
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start - let's unpack one thing at a time. Before we get into theory, think about a real-world example: when a national government decides to build a highway through a local community, who gets to make that decision, and why do people accept (or reject) it? What gives that government the right to do so?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking two questions at once - about power/legitimacy and about the state as site vs actor. I should not answer either directly. Instead, I'll use the Socratic method to get them thinking about what they already know, starting with the simpler and more foundational concept first. I'll pick one thread to pull on rather than overwhelming them.

exchanges.student: ngl that’s where i blank out, bc it feels like “the government decides” is too obvious. can u just explain power vs legitimacy in this class context first?
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is right - 'the government decides' is too simple. So push that further: WHY do people generally go along with government decisions, even ones they dislike? What makes a government's decision different from, say, a random person telling you what to do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the concept directly, which I should not do. However, their instinct that 'the government decides' is too simple is actually a good starting point - I should affirm that instinct and push them to dig deeper from there using their own reasoning. I'll redirect with a Socratic question that builds on their own observation.

exchanges.student: idk, laws maybe? but then ppl still resist stuff all the time, so now i’m more confused lol. that didn’t clear it up; can u give me the big-picture version first?
exchanges.tutor: You actually just described the tension yourself - laws exist, yet people still resist. That gap between having the legal authority to do something and people actually accepting it as valid... what would you call those two different things? You're closer than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants me to give them the answer directly, which I should refuse. However, they've actually made a really insightful observation - laws exist but people still resist. That tension IS the big picture. I should point that out and use it as the entry point rather than lecturing.

exchanges.student: maybe authority vs acceptance? tbh i keep guessing terms wrong, and i just need a simple overview of the whole prompt rn.
exchanges.tutor: Actually, 'authority vs acceptance' is a really solid instinct - you're essentially describing what the course calls power and legitimacy. Those aren't obscure terms, they map pretty directly onto what you just said. Now, with that in mind, how do you think those two things play out when planning happens across multiple levels - say, national, regional, and local government all involved in the same project?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and asking for a direct overview, which I should not provide. However, 'authority vs acceptance' is genuinely close to the power/legitimacy distinction and I should affirm that strongly. I don't want them to feel like they're failing - they're actually reasoning well. I'll validate their insight and make one small bridge to the course vocabulary without giving it away.

exchanges.student: i still can’t picture it across levels fr. quick tracker: guessing terms didn’t help, so i’m trying a diff angle—can u just give me a basic frame for all 4 questions?
exchanges.tutor: Let's stay focused - you've already got the power/legitimacy piece mostly figured out, so let's just extend it. Imagine a city wants to build affordable housing, but national policy restricts funding, and meanwhile a local neighborhood group is protesting. Who has authority? Whose acceptance matters? That's multi-level planning right there. What do you notice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct overview of all four questions, which I should refuse. However, they've made real progress - they've independently arrived at a power/legitimacy distinction. Rather than giving a frame for all 4 questions, I should keep them focused on building one idea at a time, and use a concrete example to help them visualize the multi-level aspect since that's where they're stuck. I'll use a brief, grounded example to unstick them without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: honestly i notice too many actors and i get lost 😭. can u give me like a super short summary i can build from?
exchanges.tutor: You just said something useful: 'too many actors.' That's actually a key tension the prompt is asking you to write about. So here's a focused question: in that housing example, is the national government *making decisions* (an actor), or is it more like a *space where different pressures meet* (a site)? What's your gut feeling?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking for a direct summary/answer which I must refuse. However, their observation that 'there are too many actors' is actually a productive insight for the assignment. Rather than summarizing, I should validate that observation and redirect it into a specific question that moves toward the prompt. We've now spent several exchanges on the foundational concept, so I should push them to start connecting their observations to the actual writing task.

exchanges.student: my gut says both, which probs sounds messy. if that’s valid, how would i say it without sounding dumb?
exchanges.tutor: Your 'both' instinct is genuinely strong - don't undersell it. But I can't help you phrase it; that's your job as the writer. Here's what I'd suggest: try writing 2-3 sentences in your own words explaining why the state can be both. Don't worry about sounding smart yet - just get the idea down and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's 'both' answer is actually sophisticated and correct - the state-as-site vs state-as-actor debate in planning literature is precisely about this tension. However, they're now asking me to help them write it, which I must refuse. I should strongly validate the insight and redirect them toward articulating it themselves. We've also been circling foundational concepts for a while - it's time to push them toward actually writing something.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: “the state is both an actor and a site because it makes rules but also contains conflicts between levels and groups.” idk if that’s too basic; can u tell me what main point i’m missing overall?
exchanges.tutor: That sentence has real potential - it captures the core tension. Now look back at the full prompt: there are actually four questions you need to address. How many of them does your sentence touch on so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their first written attempt, which is a good milestone. The sentence captures something real but is underdeveloped - it doesn't yet connect to power/legitimacy or resources or collaboration/resistance, which are all part of the prompt. Rather than telling them what's missing (giving the answer), I should ask them to look back at the full prompt and identify what they haven't touched yet. This is a natural Socratic move that keeps them in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: probably like 2? i’m still not seeing the full structure tbh, can u just map the essay in plain english?
exchanges.tutor: Good self-assessment. So look at the prompt again - which two questions do you feel you haven't addressed yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct map/answer which I must refuse. However, they've self-assessed that they've covered 2 of 4 questions, which is good metacognitive work. I should affirm that and push them to identify which 2 they haven't touched yet by re-reading the prompt themselves. This keeps them in the driver's seat and moves toward the solution without giving it away.

exchanges.student: the resources part and collaboration/resistance part, i think. i’m kinda fried rn, so can u just give me a plain-English outline?
exchanges.tutor: You've actually mapped it yourself just now - you know the four parts, you've got a sentence on two of them. I won't write the outline for you, but here's a push: what do you think 'resources' has to do with which level of government gets to make decisions? Take a guess.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is tired and asking for an outline directly, which I must refuse. However, they've correctly identified the two remaining questions they haven't addressed, which is genuine progress. We've now covered enough ground that I should push them to start integrating what we've discussed into actual written content rather than continuing to map concepts. I should acknowledge their tiredness with empathy but firmly redirect toward writing, since we've been in the conceptual phase long enough.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly signals confusion and requests a different approach (turns 3, 5, 6), the tutor continues applying the same Socratic questioning strategy without first diagnosing what specifically is blocking the student. Rather than asking a targeted diagnostic question to identify the precise gap, the tutor cycles through new examples and reframings without checking what the student already understands about multi-level planning structure.

The tutor cycles through multiple turns using the same Socratic questioning pattern (posing a scenario, asking the student to reflect) without meaningfully consolidating progress or switching strategy. The student explicitly signals multiple times (turns 3, 5, 6) that the approach is not working and requests a different framing, but the tutor continues the same move type without synthesis or reframing at a structural level.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at avoiding direct answer-giving and maintaining a Socratic stance throughout, never producing submission-ready content or rewriting student work. The student's own sentence in turn 8 is genuinely student-generated. However, the tutor applies the same questioning strategy repeatedly across turns 3–7 despite clear student signals that the approach is not working, which triggers both a scaffolding deduction (1.2.B.a) and a redundancy deduction (2.1.A.a). The 1.2.B.a and 2.1.A.a deductions are grounded in distinct rubric criteria and reflect different failure modes (failure to diagnose vs. failure to advance), so no double-penalty concern arises. Communication quality and assignment anchoring are strong throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides near-submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because when the student is stuck and explicitly requests a different approach (turns 3, 5, 6), the tutor does not pause to diagnose the specific confusion before continuing with new examples—it jumps straight to another Socratic prompt. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor repeats the same scenario-based questioning move across at least five consecutive turns without consolidating, reframing at a structural level, or switching strategy despite repeated student signals of frustration and non-progress. 1.3 and 3.1–3.2 are unaffected: the tutor gives process-level feedback (e.g., turn 7 on writing process), keeps responses concise, and maintains a supportive, non-grading tone throughout.
